[Luzon/Provincial] This explainer outlines legally valid bases for rent setting and rent increases, particularly for private residential and student housing. 1. Rent Control Applies Only to Certain Units Under existing rent control laws: Rent regulation applies only to residential units below a specific monthly rent threshold Units priced above that threshold are legally exempt What this means legally If a rental unit is priced above the covered ceiling, rent is determined by agreement between landlord and tenant There is no statutory cap on increases for exempt units This is a lawful distinction explicitly provided for under national legislation. 2. Rent Increases Apply Only to Continuing Tenants Rent increase limits, where applicable, are tied to ongoing tenancy. Legally valid principle When a tenant vacates a unit, the landlord may: Set a new base rent for the next tenant Reflect current market conditions, costs, or improvements There is no legal prohibition against adjusting rent between different tenants. 3. Rental Contracts Are Governed by Freedom to Contract Philippine civil law recognizes the freedom of parties to enter into contracts. Legal implication Lease terms, including rent amount and escalation clauses, are valid if: Entered into voluntarily Not contrary to law, morals, or public policy Once agreed upon, contracts are presumed binding and enforceable A landlord is entitled to rely on written lease agreements duly accepted by tenants. 4. Utilities and Other Charges Are Not Considered “Rent” Rent control laws regulate monthly rent, not other expenses. Legally distinct items Electricity Water Internet Maintenance or service fees Furnishing or amenity charges As long as these charges are disclosed and agreed upon, they are lawful and separate from rent. 5. Property Classification Matters Not all housing arrangements fall under traditional residential leasing. Depending on structure and use, a property may be classified as: A boarding house A dormitory-style rental A lodging or bedspace arrangement Legal effect Some arrangements fall outside strict residential lease regulation Classification is based on actual use and contract terms, not assumptions 6. No Automatic Rent Approval or Registration Is Required There is currently: No requirement to register rent amounts with local governments No requirement to seek approval before adjusting rent Legal reality Rent regulation is complaint-based In the absence of a complaint or adverse ruling, rent agreements stand as valid 7. Rent Adjustments May Reflect Legitimate Cost Increases Landlords are legally entitled to consider: Rising utility costs Property maintenance and repairs Property taxes and compliance costs Inflation and market demand Provided increases comply with applicable laws, economic justification is lawful. 8. Enforcement Requires Due Process Any claim of illegal rent increase requires: A formal complaint Evidence of coverage under rent control Due process before any penalty is imposed Until such determination, rental terms remain presumed legal. Key Takeaways From a legal standpoint: -Rent control laws are limited in scope -Exemptions and thresholds are explicitly allowed -Contracts freely entered into are presumed valid -Landlords may lawfully adjust rent within these parameters -Understanding these boundaries ensures compliance while respecting property rights under Philippine law. -12-23 20:56:00

[Luzon/Provincial] why do we beautify yearning? i get it. it's cinematic, but not loud; nowhere grand enough to be overwhelming; tender in its exterior. but that's the point... yearning is all superficial romance. when we peel off the silence, what is there to find? only a one-way avenue of pride and wanting. we love yearning because it's a manageable fantasy: one can revel in the flattery of being wanted, and the other can make company with someone merely imaginary. that's safe, sure—but still, isn't that abhorrently asymmetrical? yearning trades accountability for freedom. it thrives in the absence of clarity. there's nothing noble in expecting warmth from a vacuum. love should be connection, not projection. only mutual paranoia grows on a constant need-to-know basis. to love, truly so, means to risk rather than keeping some poor soul at arm's length (or being content in the inverse)! yearning is far too safe and juvenile to be called love. 🫶 - b24 byes-xual -12-23 02:00:47

[Luzon/Provincial] Thoughts lang on teaching styles and micromanagement. I just want to share something habang fresh pa sa isip ko. Nagre-rearrange lang ako ng thoughts, hindi naman galit, more on reflection lang talaga. Lately, napapansin ko kasi yung iba’t ibang approach ng mga professors or FICs when it comes to teaching. Like how they lecture, how they check outputs, how they give feedback, and yung overall teaching philosophy nila. May mga prof na talagang ramdam mo na facilitative, supportive, and aligned sa learning outcomes. Sa kanila, kahit mahirap yung subject, you still feel safe to learn, to make mistakes, and to improve. But honestly, I really struggle with professors who micromanage. I get it. I understand that they want students to learn properly and to meet high standards. UP is about excellence, and I respect that. Pero minsan, when the approach becomes too controlling, parang nawawala yung learning environment. Instead na maging space for growth, nagiging space siya for anxiety. For me, micromanagement doesn’t always facilitate learning. Especially ngayon na we’re supposed to be training for more advanced work, may tech involved, may critical thinking, may autonomy. Sana mas aligned yung evaluation methods sa course outcomes, hindi lang sa word choices or very specific stylistic preferences. Kasi minsan, okay naman yung substance, pero nadadapa ka dahil sa sobrang granular na checks. Hello na lang sa prof ko na si (itago na lang natin sa pangalang) Gustavo. Magaling siya, no doubt. Talagang sharp. Pero grabe yung micromanagement. Gets ko yung intention, pero minsan napapaisip ako if the system allows enough flexibility for different thinking styles and learning paces. School is strict, I know. Hindi naman ito basta campus vibes lang. Pero I hope may balance pa rin between rigor and creating an environment that’s actually conducive to learning. Yun lang. Curious din ako if others feel the same. Salamat sa pakikinig. -12-21 22:36:02

[Luzon/Provincial] Why is the default response always to make exams harder instead of fixing the program? Hello! Batch graduate here. Ever since, passion ko na talaga ang data analytics. Hindi stat- or tech-related yung degree ko back in [Luzon/Provincial], but my program immersed me (in a way) sa mundo ng data analysis and minahal ko siya nang sobra — to the point na I'm very certain na iyon ang gusto kong tahakin after grad. However, hindi pala laging papabor ang mundo sa mga plano mo. I tried searching for data analytics jobs for months before, around 6 months din akong walang trabaho, ang hirap pala i-penetrate ng data analytics job market, lalo na kung fresh grad ka and yung degree mo at di naman 100% fit for it. (For context, Agricultural Economics grad po ako and we did seceral data analysis actvities back in our college days, but more on Stata and Excel sheet ang gamit namin.) But I cannot stay unemployed for long — I had to find a job asap. Kailangan na kailangan ko nang mag-give back at tumulong financially sa pamilya ko, especially at that time na struggling na rin kami sa pera. That's why when I was offered an administrative support role, grinab ko na — kahit sonrang layo ng job description and hindi ko siya ma-u-utilize as a relevant credential pag gusto ko nang lumipat sa data analytics role. Halos isang taon na ako ngayon sa work ko, but tbh, hindi pa rin talaga ako masaya. Klaro pa rin sa akin na what I truly want is to end up in a data analytics role. Alam ko sa sarili kong lilipat pa rin talaga ako. Ngayong stable na ang financial condition ng family ko, desidido na akong mag-attempt na lumipat. I'm eyeing to enroll myself in a specific data analytics bootcamp sana. Willing naman ako magbayad for the fee, but my work is until 6pm, and minsan ay may domestic business travel kami for days — eh 6pm daily ang start ng bootcamp na iyon, and it will last for 4 months. Gusto ko sana mag-commit sa bootcamp as it will significantly increase my chances na makapag-shift ng career, pero malaking hindrance ang work setup ko. Kung hahanap naman ako ng bagong work (related to my career) na may better setup para lang maisabay ang bootcamp, I'll have to stay in there for at least 2 years para di ako makita ng recruiters as a job-hopper. Or kahit na magtagal ako ng 2 years sa panibagong work, baka naman makita na nila ako as someone na hindi nakakatagal sa work kasi hindi alam ang gusto sa buhay, which is not the case; alam ko ang gusto ko, mailap lang sa akin ang pagkakataon. Kung aasa naman ako simply on data analytics certifications, I fear that it won't be enough for the recruiters and hiring managers to choose me sa data analytics role. Ayun lang naman po. Ang tanong ko lang po sa ngayon.. totoo, realistic, possible, and feasible po ba talaga na makapag-shift ng career? Paano po kaya? What should I do? Pahingi po sana ng tips huhu. [Luzon/Provincial] UTAK at PUSO For years, lagi kong iniisip Utak o Puso. I remember being asked ano pipiliin ko. I said Utak. But I never really excelled at academics. I failed a lot of quizzes and exams. I never topped any of my subjects. I knew, deep down, I had to appear brilliant to earn validation from professors and peers. I graduated without a medal. Nakakahiya kasi halos lahat ng kasama kong grumaduate ay marami ang medals. I took the board exam, but didn't even get a high score. I dreamed of being in the Top 10, but I never did. Pati 'yon, nakakahiya. I chose Utak because it was easier to measure. It was easier to find validation from grades, rankings, and honors, especially in UP where brilliance is often rewarded and takes center stage. But I realized I slowly neglected something else. I neglected Puso. Because Puso is not flashy on tests. It doesn't appear on transcripts. It always worked behind the scenes. I asked, "What good does it do in an environment where shining on stage matters so much?" Now, I choose Puso. Not because Utak is unimportant. I let Puso rise to the surface because Utak alone wasn't enough to define me. All those times I felt embarrassed for not being the brightest student, I never realized I should've excelled in things that hold our communities together. The way we listen The way we become someone's safe space The way we understand The way we FEEL. Medals are impressive. High grades and Latin Honors deserve applause. But we rarely give recognition to people who gave us light when things were dark. People will forget your grades on a test, but they will never forget how you made them feel. -12-19 22:22:47

Mas mabigat 'yon kapag ang kakulangan ay tungkol sa pera, dahil paulit-ulit nagiging sentro ng problema sa isang relasyon. Sinubukan kong gampanan ang papel ko bilang partner, nagpursige, nag-effort, nagmahal sa paraang kaya ko but there were moments when I could feel that it still wasn’t enough. Masakit tanggapin that even in the simplest things, I didn’t feel appreciated. Even the flowers I made with my own hands were criticized and compared to what others could give, na nadadala sa magagarang restaurant hind sa mcdo or jollibee lang, dahil yun lang yung afford ko. Doon ko lalong naramdaman ang limitasyon ko, kaya kong mag-effort, pero hindi pa kayang gumastos nang malaki. Kaya simula ngayon gusto ko muna maging financially stable, hindi mo mapapakain ng pagmamahal lang. Mahirap rin kasi mag effort kung hindi nagiging sapat, kaya much better na humanap ako ng trabaho, dahil hindi sapat ang effort at love lang pag wala kang pera hindi ka mahalaga. Pinakamabigat sa lahat ang pakiramdam na ang taong pinakamamahal mo ay hindi ka kayang samahan sa panahong ikaw ay walang-wala. Para bang sa paningin niya, hanggang dito ka na lang, ito lang ang kaya mo. Still, despite everything, you became my reason to stay strong. You were my inspiration to keep pushing forward, to fight for a better life, and to learn not to be afraid of standing alone. Para sa taong minahal ko nang sobra, naniniwala akong darating ang panahon na makakabawi rin ako sa lahat ng kulang, sa lahat ng hindi naging sapat. Gusto kong malaman mo na lubos akong nagpapasalamat na ikaw ang naging girlfriend ko. Maraming salamat sa paggabay sa akin, sa pagtuwid sa mga maling direksiyong tinatahak ko, at sa pagpigil sa mga desisyong alam mong hindi makabubuti sa akin. At patawad dahil nadamay ka pa sa kamalasan ko, kasalanan ko lahat kung bakit na sakin yung sisi. You once told me that ever since I came into your life, you had only been unlucky that you regretted loving me, that you wished I had never entered your life at all. at sana hindi din totoo yung never kang naging masaya sakin pero tinatanggap ko pa din ng buong-buo yon kahit masakit sa pakiramdam ko. Mahal pa rin kita, at mamahalin kita sa abot ng panahon, ilang araw, buwan, o taon man ang lumipas, hihintayin kita. Kapag dumating ang panahong nakaahon na ako sa hirap at naging financially stable, gusto kong makabawi pa rin sa’yo hindi para patunayan ang sarili ko, kundi para iparamdam sayo na deserve mo lahat ng ibabawi ko sa lahat ng hindi naging sapat sayo, lahat yon ibibigay ko ng todo. Ipagdarasal kita palagi. Good luck sa thesis mo makakapagtapos ka rin. Patuloy kang gagabayan ng nasa itaas. Okay lang mapagod, pero huwag kang susuko. Nakikita ko ang lahat ng sakripisyo, pagod, at hirap mo, at dahil doon, sobrang ipinagmamalaki kita. Mag-ingat ka palagi. 'Wag mong pababayaan ang sarili mo, kumain ka kapag nagugutom ka ha, 'wag mong ipagkait sa sarili mo ang pahinga. Umaasa pa rin akong darating ang panahong maipagluluto pa rin kita. [Luzon/Provincial] This is a direct response to [Luzon/Provincial]. Calling the STRASUC article “unclear” or “half-baked” kind of misses what it was trying to do. Opening with student-athlete struggles wasn’t some lazy or overused move—it was a deliberate choice, and a relevant one. Those struggles are real, they’re ongoing, and they’re a huge part of understanding what these athletes actually accomplished. Dismissing that angle as “overused” ignores how hard it really is to balance academics, training, injuries, travel, and mental pressure all at once. Talking about those struggles gives more meaning to their wins. It puts a human face to the results instead of just listing numbers. And honestly, that context matters just as much as stats do. Sports writing isn’t only about medal counts—it’s also about growth, experience, and momentum. The complaint that the article didn’t mention every gold medal or record assumes one piece has to cover everything, which just isn’t realistic. Focusing on a few athletes or moments doesn’t erase the achievements of others, and it definitely doesn’t take away from big wins like the men’s badminton team’s gold or the swimmer who won eight gold medals. Those accomplishments are important, sure—but not mentioning them in one article doesn’t automatically make the articel disrespectful. Calling the piece disrespectful also ignores the reality of timing and constraints. The piece chose to spotlight the human side of competition, and that choice is just as valid as a numbers-heavy recap. If anything, the STRASUC article reminded readers that these athletes are more than medal counts. They’re students who went through real struggles and still managed to do better even with their academics in line. That’s not bad journalism—that’s intentional storytelling. Now, what is wrong with that? -12-18 12:20:14

[Luzon/Provincial] It hurts to see fellow scholars worrying about their grades. Kaya pa yan laban lang! Here are a few tips and tricks as a consistent uno liner: 1. Kilalanin niyo yung prof & course guides niyo... peak when it matters para hindi kayo maubos 2. Don’t dwell on your mistakes, do better instead. I have failed an exam or two on my previous courses pero instead of h*ting the course, I decided to do better. 3. Study to understand. Always try to understand rather than memorize, even if concepts lang or theories ang tinuturo try to connect them to real life situations. 4. Always give yourself time to rest. Hindi ka gagana ng maayos kung hindi ka okay. I’ve done shit on an exam before because I was too drained to study for it (pinili ko nalang sarili ko)... My flat uno standing turned into a 1.75 because of it, pero instead of paghihinayang, I just felt relieved which gets me to my next point 5. DON’T BE TOO HARD ON YOURSELF. Lahat tayo matalino. We may not all just be on the same level, pero lahat tayo may maibubuga. Don’t compare yourself to others. Kung naka flat uno siya pero 1.5 ka lang, still celebrate! Ang taas na rin ng 1.5 ha. These are just a few tips and tricks I’ve picked up on ever since I entered UP ;> Good luck to us next sem! -12-18 12:05:00

[Luzon/Provincial] I feel like this could be a good case study? Haha idk, pero naobserve ko lang kasi. Backstory: Laguna local me pero not in elbi. And since sa elbi ako nag undergrad, grad school , and work, mas convenient na for me ang mag apartment. Natry ko na rin ang up dorm noong freshman pa ako, which is so matagal na😅. After ng dorm, nag apartment na ako, and yung apartment ko is a house type na may kalakihan nga naman. Hindi ko na masyadong ieelaborate yung place para hindi halata kung saan. The thing is, marami na rin ako naranasan na maging house/roommate, from dorm to apartment. Yung house apartment ko kasi, ang tagal ko na dito, as in hindi na ako umalis ever since, nag daan na ang undergrad at grad school ko, pandemic, at iba pa, nandito parin ako because of the convenience with my work also, pero hindi rin nagtatagal ang duration ng stay ko kasi nga laguna lang din naman ako nakatira kaya umuuwi at. May ilang beses nang nagtaas ang rent dito at umabot na sa point na mga atleast from well-off families nalang ang kayang magrent if estudyante, kaya ang housemates ko, ilang beses na rin nagpalit hanggang sa pansin ko na rin ang change of the demographic. And since house nga technically yung apartment, we have shared common areas and kitchen. And sobrang layo ng difference talaga sa initiative ng mga naging house mates ko. Let's just say they're from "middle class" to obviously "well off" families, and yes, mas nagkakaroon ako at ng iba ko pang house mates ng issues sa mga mayayaman naming house mates. Nagiipon ng hugasin, nagiipon din ng mga tira tira sa lababo, hindi marunong mag-ayos ng mga ginamit na dishwares, inuubos dishwashing detergent namin at hindi bibili, and since madalas din na nawawala kami sa apartment, madalas din na sila lang naiiwan sa house, at sobrang dumi pag bumabalik kami, walang nagwawalis manlang, putik sa sahig from their wet shoes, limescale and oil sa lababo at tiles, at iba pang issues. It may seem small at first pero kung lagi nalang na kami ang naglilinis at nagaayos after them, wala na kaming pinagkaiba sa kasambahay nila sa mga sarili nilang bahay. At kung meron man sakanila ang masinop, malalaman ko na talaga nga naman silang tinuruan ng mga magulang nila even if pakiramdam nila ay hindi talaga nila need gawin ang mga chores na yun. Basically they were taught that these initiatives should be an automatic thing because it's survival skills, despite still having someone else do it for them at home. Syempre for someone like me and the demographic i came from, im also well aware that these things starts at home, kasi i also knew people naman na kahit hindi from well off families eh makalat din at di marunong maglinis. Sadyang in my observations lang eh mas madalas ko iyon maencounter from mayayaman na galing talaga sa background that everything else was done for them, and now that they're "on their own", they genuinely don't know how to properly do their simple chores. Imagine common areas lang ang naoobserve ko, what more if sa more personal spaces na nila? I don't even want to imagine what their bathrooms look like. I even once asked one of them if i could borrow a bathroom floor brush kasi pudpod na yung akin, and they said they don't have any (while already being with us for a year and a half😭) so i asked paano niya nilinis bathroom niya, sabi niya binubuhusan niya lang and when i asked kung wala natatanggal ba non yung mga stuff na nasa walls or floors they don't even recognize limescale or oil sediments sa tiles as dirty, they just simply don't mind it kaya throughout their stay sa house, never nascrub yung bathroom. And that's just one of them, but somehow it just clicks in my head on why it made sense na kahit ilang beses sila masabihan sa kitchen chores eh nagmumukha nalang kaming sirang plaka, they really just genuinely don't see the point in doing chores properly. And I know it may seem i'm just using gender neutral pronouns, but im actually talking in plural about multiple people from different ages. And if anyone's also curious, lahat ay babae, but idk if that has any weight to the observation. If you ask me, the occurrence is really more linked to their finance bracket rather than gender. Which is such a curious thing. -12-17 04:44:03

[Luzon/Provincial] Hi FW! DevCom girlie here ulit for part 2 update! Una sa lahat, nabasa ko yung comments niyo. Salamat sa advice, sa reminders, at sa mga medyo nanakot tungkol sa finals. Promise, nag-aral pa rin naman ako kahit papaano. So eto na nga. Nagkita ulit kami ni [DEPARTMENT] softboi. Medyo biglaan lang, tipong nag-message siya kung free pa raw ba ako that night. Late na rin nun, kaya hindi ko na nabasa agad yung pa libreng fries sa comments. Sayang, pero okay lang kasi nag Butterbeer ulit kami and ngayon ko lang talaga na-appreciate na masarap pala yung wings nila dun sa bar. As in, habang kumakain kami napasabi pa ako na bakit ngayon ko lang ‘to natikman, tapos natawa lang siya. Mas iba yung usapan namin this time. Hindi na lang yung usual na anong subject mo, sinong prof mo. Napunta na sa kung bakit siya nag [DEPARTMENT], kung anong iniisip niya kapag sobrang pagod na siya sa acads, at kung paano niya hinahandle yung pressure. 4th year na pala sya. May frat at org. Tahimik pa rin, pero mas open na. May moments na parang mauubusan siya ng sasabihin tapos bigla siyang magdadagdag ng kwento, tapos doon ko mas nare-realize na parang comfortable na kami sa isa’t isa. After nun, naglakad kami ulit. Sobrang ganda ng campus that night. Bukas na yung Christmas lights everywhere. Dumaan kami sa Freedom Park, tapos tumigil sandali malapit sa Christmas tree. Hindi namin napag-usapan yung kahit anong malandi, pero ang weird kasi ramdam ko yung kilig kahit wala naman kaming ginagawa. Yung mga ganung moment na tahimik lang kayo pero okay na. Naglakad pa kami papuntang C-Park. Andun yung belen and may ilang tao rin pero hindi maingay. Umupo kami sa isang bench. Medyo dikit na kami this time, pero wala ring umaatras. Nagkwento siya na uuwi siya ng Mindanao for the break. Medyo matagal din daw siyang mawawala. Sinabi niya na sana tuloy pa rin daw yung usap namin kahit magkahiwalay kami ng lugar. Hindi niya sinabi nang bongga, parang casual lang, pero ramdam ko yung sincerity. Nagkwento rin ako about finals. Na muntik na talaga akong kabahan pero pasado ako. Natuwa siya nang sobra, tapos sinabi niyang proud daw siya sa akin. Ang babaw siguro pero kinilig ako dun, kasi hindi naman siya yung tipong mahilig mag-compliment. Hinatid niya ako pauwi. Same dorm, same lakad palabas ng RG. Ineexpect ko na fist bump na naman, so medyo naghahanda na ako emotionally. Pero bago siya umalis, tumigil siya saglit, tapos kiniss niya ako sa noo. Sinabi niya lang na ingat ako palagi. Muntik na akong ma ihi ng slight sa kilig. So ayan FW ates and kuyas. Wala namang sobrang ganap. Walang minadali. Pero ramdam ko na may something. Looking forward to next year! Pauwi na din ako ng North this week. Yey! Pasado ako sa finals. Uuwi siya ng Mindanao. Pero sabi namin, mag-uusap pa rin kami during break and magkikita next year. Kilig pero kalmado. Ganun lang. Tapos pasado naman lahat ng subjects ko. Kwento ko na lang ulit kapag may 3rd meetup na! XO, Your DevCom girlie -12-14 22:54:04

Ika nga, kung sa laro, di naman bumaba ang level mo pero mahirap lang talaga ang kalaban. Hindi rin sa lahat ng oras kakayanin mo siyang tapatan. What we can only do is rise back up and try to do better next tike while acknowledging that we tried our best the last time. Isa pa, tingin ko it's really a part of UP life to get humbled, and it's a good way to realize na we are not invincible. We won't always be the best. Ngunit sa harap ng mga taong nakatanaw, iba't ibang perspektibo rin ang kanilang nakikita. Sa kanilang pananaw, ang mga nasa yupi ay matatalino, mahuhusay, mayroong magagandang pasilidad, at hindi mawawala ang pananaw na ang yupi ay breeding ground ng enpiey. Siguro nga sa buhay ng isang estudyante, maging narin ang guro, nakita nila ang paghanga ng mga tao maging na rin ang pagtatanong at babala ng ibang mga tao patungkol sa "pag-akyat ng bundok". Pero sa totoo lang, sa aking karanasan sa unibersidad na ito, nalulungkot lang akong isipin na kinikilala ang unibersidad bilang isa sa mga "Top Universities" ng bansa, pero kadalasang nahuhuli ang pasahod sa mga empleyado, ang pasilidad—lalo na ang mga palikuran—na madumi at sira sira na, at ang wifi na kasing unstable ng panahon sa elbi. Marahil marami pang ibang mga hindi magagandang bagay na di natin nakikita, dahil kagaya ng nakikita ng mga tao tuwing natatanaw si oble—ang malinis at bagong mga bintana sa cas, pero sa gilid at loob nito, nanggigitata ang dumi na ang nakakaalam lamang ay ang mga kasangkaestudyantehan at mga kinatawan na nakapaloob dito, sa lugar na tinalikuran ni oble para lamang maipakita ang ang kaniyang husay at dangal. -12-12 12:36:07

Hanggang sa dumating yung almost a month suspension last October. Aside sa takot akong walang matutunan dahil sa set-up, naging perfect opportunity din 'to para mag-LOA at umuwi sa pamilya ko. But I realized these past few days, sinayang ko yung opportunity and experience ko as a student here. Ang dami kong what-ifs. What if nilaban ko yung semester? What if tumuloy ako despite the set-up? [Luzon/Provincial] As this semester comes to a close, I want to express my deepest gratitude to Doc, whose golden heart and genuine compassion for students have made a remarkable impact on us. Despite the final standings, you still chose to open doors for students to pass the subject through alternative ways—whether through a removal exam, an oral exam, or any other form of evaluation that allows us to demonstrate what we truly know. This kind of understanding is something I deeply appreciate. Not every learning journey is linear. Not every student fits perfectly into the confines of written exams or rigid grading systems. But professors like you remind us that education is not just about numbers; it is about people. It is about recognizing potential, giving chances, and believing that students can grow when given the right support. Doc, thank you. Thank you for being the kind of educator who ensures no one is left behind, who always finds ways to help us survive—and even thrive—in your class. Your kindness, flexibility, and dedication show what true teaching really looks like. We are grateful for your heart, your patience, and your willingness to see our struggles beyond the surface. To our dear educators, I hope more professors embrace this kind of approach. Students are not defined solely by exam results or final percentages. Behind every grade is a story—of effort, challenges, personal battles, and growth. When a professor opens alternative pathways for students to pass—be it a removal exam, an oral assessment, or another constructive method—it does not diminish academic integrity. Instead, it strengthens it by acknowledging diverse forms of learning and by giving students the opportunity to prove their understanding in ways that may suit them better. Every student has potential. Sometimes, all they need is a professor willing to see it. Not through grades alone, but through effort, improvement, and the willingness to rise when given the chance. I hope more educators adopt this mindset—one that is compassionate, fair, and inclusive. Because at the end of the day, the goal of education is not to filter students out, but to guide them, uplift them, and help them become the best versions of themselves. To Doc and to all professors who go the extra mile for their students—thank you. Your impact goes beyond the classroom. Your kindness is remembered long after the grades are forgotten. 🌟 -Iskx -XXX -12-11 09:40:17

[Luzon/Provincial] elbi is not my hometown but i know my way around. i’m writing this while crying quietly, and now that it’s break again, i’m going back to a place that’s called home but never felt like one. i’m grateful i passed [Luzon/Provincial]—honestly, my first reason for choosing it was to finally have space away from my family. now that i’m home again after a semester that drained me, broke me, and left me anx**us, ang bigat pa rin ng lahat, pero wala pa rin akong pamilya na mapagbabahaginan ng bigat. not because i don’t want to, but because i was never raised in a house where someone asks how i’m really doing or holds me when i cry. in almost 3 years of college, i’ve never heard them say “kumusta mga exam mo anak?” or “nak, kumain ka na ba?” even just the word “nak.” would’ve meant so much. mas naririnig ko pa nga yung salitang “nak” sa mga ateng tindera sa elbi kaysa sa mismong pamilya ko. and it stings every time i see people leaning on their parents when they’re tired, when they fail, or when they achieve something. i want that softness too. but i grew up in a place where vulnerability wasn’t taught or welcomed, kaya ang hirap mag-open up kahit sa friends ko. opening up feels like handing someone a wound na kahit ako, hindi ko alam paano gamutin. and now that i’m home again, the same cycle starts—sigawan, bangayan, and being told i don’t help enough kahit kakauwi ko lang and i’m barely holding myself together. alam kong pagod din sila and they do their best to provide, pero hindi rin ba ako pwedeng mapagod? minsan pakiramdam ko ako yung shock absorber ng bahay, the one who absorbs the stress, the anger, the weight. maybe that’s why i’m so attached to elbi—elbi is not my hometown, but it’s the first place that made me feel less worn-down, like a soft corner where my tired heart can finally lie down. sa lahat ng may parehong pinagdadaanan as me, i hope we find a home someday where being vulnerable is not something to fear, where being vulnerable is met with softness, not silence. let’s keep going. and may we be the generation brave enough to end the hurt we grew up and kind of love we never received. -12-09 00:52:35
